speaker: interviewee
text: December 7th, was it?

speaker: interviewer
text: We left off--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --with a message from Leslie. Would you continue?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was saying what incredible role models my parents have been. And not only have they dedicated themselves and taken from such horrific experiences, the most positive spirit, of-- I mean, the strength of human spirit, and the courage. And I guess, as we go from generation to generation, we begin to understand. Because there are just two images that I have in my mind. One, from hearing the translations that my father has done from my grandparents Klein, [? Alice ?] and [? Ludwig-- ?] that in the face of what they were dealing with.

speaker: interviewee
text: And really, when one would lose all hope and question, why go on? They still saw such courage. And they still saw the beauty of nature. To think that my grandmother would pluck a flower from the side of a road and enclose it in a letter, one has to believe that they still always looked for the positive in the world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I think of my grandparents Weissman and the story that has been told of how they faced the morning, knowing that they were going to be separated. They were married as long as I've been married. And when I hear my mother say that she doesn't know how they faced the morning, other than with the love and the courage. They spoke only of the positive things that had happened in their lives. Obviously, our parents each had in their own parents incredible role models. And once again, what role models we have had from generation to generation, we have inherited a  legacy that we certainly hope that we, too, can pass onto our children. And that they, in turn, can make this world a better place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I am relatively new to the family. I'm a little over 25 years married to Vivian. And I come from a very assimilated German-Jewish background. I remember growing up that I really never knew anybody that could--could basically state that all four of their grandparents were born in the United States. But that was the case with-- with my family, quite assimilated. My mother's side was relatively religious. My father's side was not. And as a result of that, I did read Gerda's book. And of course, knew about the Holocaust and the atrocities. I'd read long before as a young teenager, long before I joined the family at age 23. But I had no-- you just can't understand the Holocaust without a-- a personal-- bringing it down to a personal level, personalization of it. And, of course, joining the family and-- and learning from Gerda and Kurt's experiences the enormity of what occurred just to one family. And Gerda always states that, you know, many of the most talented and bravest are-- are not here. So she's a special spokesperson for those. It was really a mind boggler for me. Because basically, I knew very few survivors. And they were very, very unwilling to talk. Gerda was really among one of the first and really a leader in that area. And I'm going to pick up on something Leslie said-- to talk throughout the country and be really in the vanguard of Holocaust education. Many, many years ago [? and ?] leave the family when they were young, and then come home, I'm struck with one thing as an outsider, that new one of the family, is the absolute normalcy of the family when I came over to date and spend time as a-- as a boyfriend, and then part of the family. It was not anything other than a place of joy, laughter, good humor, and warmth, as opposed to anything that would be perhaps morose or looking back at the dark clouds of a prior year. And that is possibly the best compliment that I could give to both Gerda and Kurt. Because even though Gerda-- Gerda lost directly, Kurt certainly-- the years that she was interred, Dad's story is one that is just every bit as gut-wrenching in terms of the loss of his family, being so close to freedom. And you wouldn't know it to walk in the doors as a boyfriend or as a suitor. And for that, I think we owe them a debt of gratitude. Because they were able to raise, I can tell you, a normal child as a wife. And hopefully, we're doing-- we're going to try to do as well as parents to their grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. How about grandchildren? Would anybody like to say something?

speaker: interviewee
text: Andrew?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to really thank my grandparents for passing on the family tradition that they did so eloquently to us and for their amazing courage and dedication to the fact that they were survivors and spreading their ideals throughout the Jewish community and throughout the world. And I know that they share in the feeling with me that silence is-- only leads to ignorance in people. And throughout their speaking around the nation and the world, which they so often do, that they can bring about the reality of the Holocaust and only make so that it's not going to happen again. And I really want to thank them for passing down the legacy that our family has.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, Grandma and Grandpa, I just want you to know how much you mean to me, and how special you are. And I really admire-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I messed up.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jessica Simon. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hi, my name is Jessica Simon. And I'm so very lucky to have Grandma-- Grandma and Grandpa. Me and my sisters, Melissa and Julie, love them so very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, we're going to introduce some of the other members that were not able to be present today. But they've done something very special, and that is give a message via telephone machine to Gerda and to Kurt. So we're going to play those messages and pick them up on audiotape. And we will show their picture at the same time.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of our son Jim, who lives in Washington and was unable to be with us, unfortunately, today. However, he has sent a recorded message, which we are about to hear. [AUDIO PLAYBACK] - I'll start over. What makes me proudest of my mom and dad is not just that they have devoted their lives to coming to this country, to their story in order to help others, or even that they have achieved some considerable fame and celebrity in doing so. Rather, it's that they strove and achieved the ability to create a normal life for my sisters and for me. And the very ordinary nature of the life that I have lived as a child growing into adulthood has been the greatest gift that they have given me. I love them very much. [END AUDIO PLAYBACK] OK. This is a picture of our son-in-law Roger Simon, who, unfortunately, couldn't be with us tonight. However, he was good enough to send a message, which we are just going to hear now. [AUDIO PLAYBACK] - OK. From here on in, it'll be all recorded. So I'll get off. And you just talk. - And then I'll  hang up. If it doesn't work, call me back. - OK. - OK. My name is Roger Simon. And I am Leslie's husband. And, of course, Kurt and Gerda are my in-laws. I first met them now more than 20 years ago. And we developed an instant rapport. My background was that of an American-born Jew. And I had no family ties to the Holocaust, as all of my family was in the States. Of course, over the years, I've learned more and more. And I've been so impressed with their knowledge and their point of view in regards to the Holocaust in trying to have made the best out of their terrible past family history. We've become very involved. I visited Gerda's home place. And I visited the place of liberation. And you've experienced some of that through their talk. And of course, they're just the most wonderful, brightest, kindest, lovely people that anyone could meet. They're the best in-laws you could ever have. None of the in-law jokes apply to them. They're fantastic grandparents. And I love them like I love my own parents. And we're very glad to have them, and wish them continued good health. [END AUDIO PLAYBACK] This is our daughter-in-law Lynn with our son, Jim. Both of them, unfortunately, couldn't be here this evening. But Lynn as well has sent a message, which we are about to hear now. [AUDIO PLAYBACK] - Hi, Mom and Dad. It's Lynn calling. And I guess this is addressed to all the special people that are there with you today. I have written just a few sentences that I will share with you. When I first met Gerda and Kurt, Mom and Dad, and I read I remember writing a letter expressing my admiration for two people who could not only survive the atrocities of the Holocaust, but also devote their lives to insuring that no one else should suffer as they did. That letter that I wrote was addressed to Mr. and Mrs. Klein. Little did I know, five years later, I would have the privilege of calling them Mom and Dad. Their experiences have helped me appreciate what I enjoyed and valued so much-- having my own grandparents with me. My husband never knew his grandparents. Knowing what important part of my-- knowing what an important part my grandparents played in my life, I wish he, too, had had the same fond memories. Our children, however, do know and adore their grandparents. The children are too young today to understand how their grandparents have suffered. As they grow older, we will share those stories. In the meantime, they treasure each moment with Gerda and Kurt, Grandma and Grandpa. These are memories which they will share with their grandchildren. I send all my love to Mom and Dad. And we're just thrilled and anxious to hear how everything is going. Much love, Lynn. [END AUDIO PLAYBACK] This is my father, Julius Weissman. This picture was taken-- on the left is a picture of my brother, Arthur Weissman. It was taken, I believe, in the garden of our home. And I assume that Arthur was about 17 years old at that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was, as a matter of fact, in my boot. I carried it in my skiing boot throughout the war. I-- as you see, I cut it out in the form of hearts. And I had it suspended on a little bit of cardboard, which I carried in the lining of my shoe, my skiing boot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the middle is a picture of my mother, Helene Weissman. This picture was taken in the summer of 1939 in a spot called [INAUDIBLE], where I was with my mother. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is also the picture that I carried in my boot, together with a picture of my brother and my father. On the right is a picture of my father, Julius Weissman. This picture was taken in 1936, when he went to the wedding of my uncle Turkey, in Istanbul, Turkey. And this picture was taken on a boat ride on the Bosphorus. This picture as well, I carried in my boot throughout the war. This picture was taken in-- at the beginning of the war. I was 15 years old. And it was shortly after the outbreak of the war. It was taken at-- at home, still, in Bielsko. I think it was just prior to when we had to move to the basement of our home. This is a picture of my brother, Arthur Weissman. I presume it was taken when he was about 16 years old. He was born in 1919. I am not quite sure where it was taken, but I see that he is wearing the school uniform. So it must be when he was being in the gymnasium. And I presume he's about 16 on that picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture came to me through a miraculous thing. Almost 400 pictures of my family had survived and were found and was shipped to me. But some of the pictures, my uncle who lived in Turkey had them. And I'm not quite sure if this was amongst the pictures that my uncle had, or if it was a picture which survived in Bielsko, which I received much later. This is a picture of my maternal grandparents, Julia and Jacob Muckenbrunn. I presume this picture must have been taken in the early years of the marriage, which would have been in about 1885. I had that picture from my uncle who lived in Turkey. And I received this picture from him. This picture was taken in the garden of our home. And was-- my-- my mother on the extreme left. My brother Arthur is in the center. And I presume I must have been about six or seven years old. Here was an array of my toys. The girl in the background was a friend. Her name was [? Annie ?] [? Pincher ?]. I received that picture among 400 pictures, which were in-- found in a miraculous way in my hometown. This is a picture taken just before my parents' wedding, which must have been early 1919. It is taken in our garden, in the garden of our home, in front of a little sort of summer-- I don't know what you call it, like a summer home, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Julius and Helene Weissman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture, I believe my uncle had. This is a picture of my father taken during World War I in the uniform of an Austrian. I believe he was an officer. I don't know what the-- what the rank means. But it must have been taken in 19-- somewhere between 1914 and 1918. And this picture, I believe, I had from my uncle in Turkey. This is the last picture with my mother taken in the summer of 1939 in a spa called [INAUDIBLE]. My mother, Helene Weissman and I. I was just 15 years old in this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: How'd you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe I must have got it from my uncle. I'm not quite sure if it was among those pictures which I received or if my uncle had it. I really don't know which is which. This is a picture of my brother, Arthur Weissman and me. My brother is in student uniform. I presume I must have been about 12 years old here And he must be 16. He was 4 and 1/2 years older than I. This is a picture which my uncle had. I see that we both wrote on the back, sending this picture to our uncle in Turkey. This is a picture of my daughter, Leslie, and my son-in-law, Roger Simon in Las Vegas. On the extreme left is so our granddaughter, Jessica, the youngest daughter. Then Leslie and Roger. Melissa is on the floor. Unfortunately, she was not here tonight. And Julie on the extreme right, who is in college. This is our granddaughter, Alysa, the oldest granddaughter. Her name is Alysa Ullman. Alysa could not be here tonight. She is away in college and having exams right now. This is our granddaughter Julie Simon who, unfortunately, cannot be with us tonight. She is having exams. She is in college. And she's our second grandchild. This is our granddaughter Melissa Simon at her bat mitzvah two years ago. It was a very special day, because her bat mitzvah fell under sort of September, the day on which, of course, our tragedy started, was World War II beginning. And it was a wonderful climax that she would bat mitzvah on that very day which turned it into a very happy memory now. Those are our two youngest grandchildren, the two little redheads, Jennifer on the left, and Alexi-- or rather, Alexa, on the right. Two vibrant redheads who are much, much fun, our two youngest granddaughters, Jennifer and Lexi Klein. Those are five books which I have written. Over there on the extreme left is It is the story of a different child, a retarded child. It's a true story, a story of the daughter of a friend of mine, a former neighbor. The next one is It's the-- it's the biography of Edith Rosenwald Stern, daughter of Julius Rosenwald, the founder of Sears-Roebuck, who devoted her life to the betterment of human conditions and was a remarkable woman for all of that she had done to make this a better world. The next one is my own autobiography, which I wrote many years ago. And I'm-- I feel very privileged that it has remained in print for 37 years. It is presently in its 36th edition. Next to it is the book which I wrote really for my grandchildren, who are the inheritors of a new spring. And I-- I feel very fortunate to have been able to write a book and dedicate it to my grandchildren and to all inheritors of a new spring. The other book is It's sort of a fun story about adventures in Mexico of children. And-- but this is a book which teaches that happiness is not necessarily found in-- because the book has an awful lot of rainbows in it. And of course, a lot of people assume that at the end of the rainbow is a pot of gold. And this, of course, tells a different story. That actually, it is the rainbow and the love and the friendship, which is far more important. I-- we were privileged that HBO did a story on the story of my life, entitled which aired this year in 1995. And we were very fortunate to receive an Emmy this past September. This is a picture of the Emmy, of course, which now graces our home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to add one more thing. Can we just stop it? And that's the little figurine of-- Winning an Emmy, of course, is-- is a very happy moment. And it now graces our home.

speaker: interviewee
text: But equally as important, if not more so, is a tiny figurine which is a replica of the one which graces the final resting place of my friends who were killed in the Holocaust, in particularly the ones on the Death March. 95 of my friends rest in those graves in Volary where we were liberated. And this statue, is it-- as you walk into the cemetery, I think it-- it is a woman without arms. And yet, I think she almost has wings. And I think the very fact that it is gray and somehow rooted to-- to the ground, I think, symbolizes my friends who are there and who died without knowing the joy of freedom. Where the Emmy, I think of it are for us who have been fortunate and have been able to reach for the stars and have a very bright life. I feel that those two should stand together, because the one who was the [? victor, ?] in a way is fulfilling, and I hope forever will, stand here in-- in memory of those who did not have the joy of freedom and did not have children or grandchildren. And it is them that I honor most.
